label: Roaring Twenties
pitch:
In 1918 the guns of the World War go silent and a soldier-borne flu kills more Americans than the war did. Eleven years later, on a single October afternoon, the New York stock market falls off a cliff and takes the decade with it. In between is the Roaring Twenties: the first modern consumer economy, the first broadcast nation, the first car culture — and, running on the exact same calendar, the largest peacetime nativist movement in American history.

🔑 Key Facts:
• The 1918–19 influenza pandemic killed roughly 675,000 Americans in under a year — more than every U.S. combat death in the World War, World War II, Korea, and Vietnam combined
• At its 1924–25 peak the second Ku Klux Klan claimed four to five million members and effectively controlled the politics of Indiana, Oregon, and Colorado — it was a national, not a Southern, movement
• On May 21, 1927, Charles Lindbergh landed outside Paris after 33.5 hours alone in a single-engine plane, and an estimated four million people turned out for his New York ticker-tape parade — the largest crowd in the city's history to that point
• Between Black Thursday and Black Tuesday (October 24–29, 1929), the stock market lost about $30 billion in two weeks, roughly ten times the federal budget and more than the entire cost of the World War to the United States

🤔 Why It Matters: The twenties built the America that came after it. Mass production, installment credit, national advertising, the radio network, the Hollywood studio, the suburb, and the modern celebrity were all assembled in this decade and have never been disassembled. So was the modern culture war: the Scopes 'Monkey' Trial, Prohibition, immigration restriction by national origin, and the Klan were a coordinated backlash against everything the boom represented, and that argument never really ended. The decade also proved, expensively, that a stock market detached from the economy underneath it is a bubble, and that nobody in charge will say so until it bursts.

🎲 Wild Card: The decade's defining business was illegal. Prohibition didn't end drinking; it handed a multibillion-dollar industry to organized crime, which used the profits to build the modern American Mafia. Al Capone's Chicago operation grossed an estimated $60 million a year, paid no taxes, ran the city's police and aldermen, and was finally brought down not for the St. Valentine's Day Massacre but for failing to file a tax return — the one law a bootlegger could not buy his way around.

details.label: Background
details.text: The United States entered the World War in April 1917 and shipped two million men to France by the armistice of November 1918. The country that won the war was a great industrial power with no plan for what to do with itself afterward.

details.label: What Happened
details.text: An influenza pandemic killed roughly 675,000 Americans. Wartime price controls came off into a brutal inflation, four million workers struck in 1919, and the Bolshevik Revolution abroad triggered the First Red Scare, the Palmer Raids, and mass deportations. White mobs attacked Black communities in the Red Summer of 1919 and destroyed the Greenwood district of Tulsa in 1921.

details.label: The Bigger Picture
details.text: The boom that gave the decade its name was preceded by two years of plague, strikes, race massacres, and political terror, and a sharp depression in 1920–21. The 'return to normalcy' Warren Harding campaigned on in 1920 was a promise to make the previous three years stop.

details.label: Background
details.text: Warren Harding, an amiable Ohio newspaper publisher and senator, won the 1920 election in the largest popular-vote landslide to that point on a platform of doing less. He filled the cabinet with a mix of genuine talents and Ohio cronies.

details.label: What Happened
details.text: Andrew Mellon began cutting the top income-tax rate, the economy recovered fast, and immigration restriction began with the 1921 Emergency Quota Act. Meanwhile the 'Ohio Gang' looted the government: the Teapot Dome oil-lease bribery became the worst federal corruption scandal before Watergate. Harding died suddenly in August 1923 before most of it surfaced.

details.label: The Bigger Picture
details.text: Harding's death turned a mediocre, scandal-ridden presidency into a martyrdom and handed a clean, silent Vermonter the White House at the exact moment the boom took off.

details.label: Background
details.text: Calvin Coolidge took the oath by lamplight on his father's Vermont farm, said as little as possible, and let business run. 'The chief business of the American people is business.'

details.label: What Happened
details.text: The Mellon tax cuts, mass production, installment credit, electrification, and national advertising produced the first modern consumer economy. Cars, radios, movies, and appliances reached the mass market. The 1924 Johnson–Reed Act slammed the immigration door, the second Klan peaked and then collapsed, and the Scopes trial staged the culture war on the radio.

details.label: The Bigger Picture
details.text: Real, broad, and unequal: farmers, Black Americans, and the coal and textile regions never got the boom, and the prosperity was increasingly running on debt. Coolidge 'did not choose to run' in 1928 and left office before the bill came due.

details.label: Background
details.text: A generation came home from the war, the Nineteenth Amendment enfranchised women, the Great Migration moved more than a million Black Americans north, and radio and records carried the new music everywhere at once.

details.label: What Happened
details.text: Jazz went national through Louis Armstrong, Duke Ellington, and Bessie Smith. The Harlem Renaissance produced Hughes, Hurston, McKay, and Locke's 'The New Negro,' Marcus Garvey's mass movement, and the NAACP's anti-lynching campaign. F. Scott Fitzgerald named the age and Hemingway and the Lost Generation rewrote American prose.

details.label: The Bigger Picture
details.text: The decade's permanent cultural legacy was made by the people the decade's politics were trying to suppress — Black musicians, immigrant filmmakers, and a generation of writers in open revolt against the official optimism.

details.label: Background
details.text: After the 1926 collapse of the Florida land boom, speculative money moved to Wall Street. Herbert Hoover, the celebrated engineer-administrator, won the 1928 election in another landslide promising the 'final triumph over poverty.'

details.label: What Happened
details.text: Stock prices roughly doubled in two years on a flood of borrowed 'margin' money and leveraged investment trusts. The market peaked on September 3, 1929, wobbled, and then crashed on Black Thursday, Black Monday, and Black Tuesday in late October.

details.label: The Bigger Picture
details.text: The Crash did not by itself cause the Great Depression, but it ended the decade's defining illusion: that the boom was permanent and the prosperity general. What the Depression and the next war reshaped is the subject of the narratives that follow.

events.label: Armistice ends the World War
events.description: At 11 a.m. on November 11, 1918, the guns of the Western Front fell silent. Roughly two million American soldiers had been shipped to France since 1917, and about 53,000 died in combat. The sudden end of war spending plunged the economy into an uncertain demobilization.

events.label: 1918 influenza pandemic
events.description: A lethal strain of influenza, spread along troop movements, killed roughly 675,000 Americans between 1918 and 1919 — more than all U.S. combat deaths in the World War. It killed healthy young adults at unusual rates and overwhelmed cities in weeks.

events.label: Wilson's Fourteen Points & Versailles
events.description: President Woodrow Wilson went to Paris to build a League of Nations on his Fourteen Points. The 1919 Treaty of Versailles included the League, but the U.S. Senate rejected the treaty in 1919–20, and America never joined.

events.label: Eighteenth Amendment ratified
events.description: The Eighteenth Amendment, ratified in January 1919, banned the manufacture, sale, and transport of intoxicating liquor nationwide. It was the only constitutional amendment ever passed to restrict a personal freedom and the only one later repealed.

events.label: Seattle General Strike
events.description: Some 65,000 Seattle workers walked out in February 1919 in the first general strike in U.S. history. It was peaceful and brief but, amid the Bolshevik scare abroad, was widely portrayed as the beginning of an American revolution.

events.label: The 1919 strike wave
events.description: About four million workers — one in five — struck in 1919 as wartime wage controls came off into runaway inflation. The Great Steel Strike of 350,000 men and a national coal strike were broken with troops, injunctions, and a Red-revolution press campaign.

events.label: Boston Police Strike
events.description: Boston's police struck for the right to unionize in September 1919, and looting followed. Massachusetts Governor Calvin Coolidge broke the strike and declared 'there is no right to strike against the public safety,' a single sentence that made him a national figure.

events.label: Red Summer of 1919
events.description: White mobs attacked Black communities in more than two dozen cities in the summer of 1919, including Chicago, Washington, and Elaine, Arkansas. Returning Black veterans fought back, and the NAACP's anti-lynching campaign gained urgency.

events.label: Chicago race riot of 1919
events.description: After a Black teenager was stoned and drowned for crossing an unofficial color line at a Lake Michigan beach, eight days of violence killed 38 people and left over a thousand Black families homeless. It was the deadliest of the Red Summer riots.

events.label: Anarchist bombing campaign
events.description: Followers of the anarchist Luigi Galleani mailed and planted bombs in 1919, including one that destroyed the front of Attorney General A. Mitchell Palmer's house. The attacks were real and frightening and were used to justify a sweeping crackdown on the left.

events.label: First Red Scare
events.description: Fear that the Russian Bolshevik Revolution would spread gripped the United States in 1919–20. Radicals, immigrants, and labor organizers were conflated with revolutionaries, and civil liberties were widely suspended in the panic.

events.label: The Palmer Raids
events.description: Attorney General A. Mitchell Palmer and his young aide J. Edgar Hoover arrested as many as 6,000 suspected radicals in raids across the country in late 1919 and January 1920, mostly without warrants. Hundreds of immigrants were deported.

events.label: The 'Soviet Ark' deportations
events.description: In December 1919 the army transport Buford carried 249 deported radicals, including the anarchist Emma Goldman, to Soviet Russia. The press called it the 'Soviet Ark'; it was the showpiece of the deportation drive.

events.label: ACLU founded
events.description: The American Civil Liberties Union was founded in 1920 in direct response to the wartime sedition prosecutions and the Palmer Raids. It became the country's leading civil-liberties litigator and would defend the Scopes teacher five years later.

events.label: Sacco and Vanzetti arrested
events.description: Two Italian immigrant anarchists, Nicola Sacco and Bartolomeo Vanzetti, were arrested in May 1920 for a payroll robbery and murder in Massachusetts. Their trial became the era's defining test of whether radicals and immigrants could get a fair hearing.

events.label: Wall Street bombing
events.description: A horse-drawn wagon packed with dynamite and iron exploded outside the J.P. Morgan bank on September 16, 1920, killing 38 people. It was the deadliest terrorist attack on U.S. soil to that point and was never solved.

events.label: Nineteenth Amendment ratified
events.description: Ratified in August 1920 after a seventy-year campaign, the Nineteenth Amendment barred denying the vote on account of sex. It enfranchised some 26 million women — though state-level disfranchisement still excluded most Black women in the South.

events.label: League of Women Voters founded
events.description: Carrie Chapman Catt founded the League of Women Voters in 1920 to turn newly enfranchised women into informed voters. The militant suffragist Alice Paul instead drafted the first Equal Rights Amendment, introduced in 1923.

events.label: Volstead Act takes effect
events.description: The Volstead Act, the enforcement law for Prohibition, took effect in January 1920 and defined 'intoxicating' as anything over 0.5 percent alcohol. It was chronically underfunded, leaving enforcement to a few thousand federal agents for the whole country.

events.label: Warren Harding elected
events.description: Ohio senator Warren G. Harding won the 1920 election in the largest popular-vote landslide to that point, promising 'not heroics but healing, not nostrums but normalcy.' It was the first presidential election in which women could vote nationwide.

events.label: KDKA goes on the air
events.description: On November 2, 1920, Westinghouse's KDKA in Pittsburgh broadcast the Harding–Cox election returns, the first commercially licensed radio broadcast in the United States. Within two years there were more than 500 stations.

events.label: Depression of 1920–21
events.description: A sharp postwar deflationary depression hit in 1920: prices and farm income collapsed, and unemployment rose toward 12 percent. The government did little, the slump ended within about 18 months, and the boom followed.

events.label: Emergency Quota Act of 1921
events.description: The 1921 Emergency Quota Act capped immigration from each country at 3 percent of that nationality's U.S. population in the 1910 census. It was the first numerical limit on European immigration and deliberately favored northern and western Europeans.

events.label: Tulsa Race Massacre
events.description: On May 31–June 1, 1921, a white mob, deputized and armed by the city, destroyed the prosperous Greenwood district of Tulsa, Oklahoma, known as 'Black Wall Street.' Estimates of the dead run from dozens to about 300; some 10,000 Black residents were left homeless.

events.label: The Great Migration
events.description: Between roughly 1916 and 1930 more than 1.5 million Black Americans left the rural South for Northern and Midwestern cities, fleeing Jim Crow and lynching and drawn by wartime factory jobs. It remade Chicago, Detroit, New York, and the politics of all three.

events.label: Rebirth of the Ku Klux Klan
events.description: Refounded at Stone Mountain, Georgia, in 1915 and turned into a mass organization by professional fundraisers, the second Klan was anti-Black, anti-Catholic, anti-Jewish, and anti-immigrant, and national in reach. Membership exploded after 1920.

events.label: Shuffle Along opens on Broadway
events.description: Written and performed by Black artists, the musical Shuffle Along ran for 504 Broadway performances in 1921 and launched a vogue for Black theater. It helped open white audiences and producers to the Harlem Renaissance about to flower uptown.

events.label: Marcus Garvey and the UNIA
events.description: Marcus Garvey's Universal Negro Improvement Association built the largest Black mass movement in American history, preaching racial pride, economic self-help, and a return to Africa. Its 1920 New York convention drew 25,000 delegates.

events.label: First Mellon tax cut
events.description: Treasury Secretary Andrew Mellon, one of the richest men in America, began cutting wartime tax rates with the Revenue Act of 1921. Over the decade the top income-tax rate fell from 73 percent to 24 percent, on the theory that lower rates would unleash investment.

events.label: Washington Naval Conference
events.description: Hosted by Secretary of State Charles Evans Hughes, the 1921–22 Washington Conference produced the first major arms-limitation treaty, capping battleship tonnage among the great powers. It reflected the decade's revulsion at the World War and turn toward disarmament.

events.label: Reader's Digest founded
events.description: DeWitt and Lila Wallace launched Reader's Digest in 1922, condensing articles for busy readers. With Time (1923) and the tabloids, it was part of a new mass-circulation media built for a hurried, national audience.

events.label: Sinclair Lewis publishes Babbitt
events.description: Sinclair Lewis's 1922 novel Babbitt satirized the conformist, booster, business-worshipping middle class of a Midwestern city. It added a word to the language and made Lewis the leading novelist-critic of the decade's commercial culture.

events.label: Fordney–McCumber Tariff
events.description: The 1922 Fordney–McCumber Tariff raised import duties to protect American manufacturers and farmers. It signaled the decade's economic nationalism and helped choke the international trade that war-debtor Europe needed to repay the United States.

events.label: Teapot Dome scandal
events.description: Interior Secretary Albert Fall secretly leased the Navy's Teapot Dome and Elk Hills oil reserves to private oilmen in exchange for about $400,000 in bribes. Exposed by a Senate investigation, it was the worst federal corruption scandal before Watergate.

events.label: The Ohio Gang
events.description: Harding's circle of poker-playing Ohio cronies sold pardons, paroles, liquor permits, and government contracts. The Justice Department, the Veterans' Bureau, and the Alien Property office were all looted; one investigator estimated the fraud at $200 million.

events.label: Veterans' Bureau scandal
events.description: Charles Forbes, head of the Veterans' Bureau, embezzled or wasted an estimated $200 million meant for disabled World War veterans through kickbacks on hospital construction and supplies. He was convicted and imprisoned.

events.label: Equal Rights Amendment introduced
events.description: Alice Paul drafted the Equal Rights Amendment and had it introduced in Congress in 1923. It split the women's movement — labor feminists feared it would void protective laws for women workers — and went nowhere for half a century.

events.label: Time magazine launched
events.description: Henry Luce and Briton Hadden launched Time in March 1923 as a weekly news digest for the busy modern reader. It pioneered a brisk, opinionated national news style and built one of the great twentieth-century media empires.

events.label: Death of President Harding
events.description: Harding died suddenly in San Francisco on August 2, 1923, probably of a heart attack, as the scandals were closing in. He was mourned as a beloved president; the looting of his administration surfaced over the following two years.

events.label: Coolidge sworn in by lamplight
events.description: Vice President Calvin Coolidge was visiting his father's farm in Plymouth Notch, Vermont, with no electricity when word of Harding's death arrived. His father, a notary, administered the oath by kerosene lamp at 2:47 a.m. on August 3, 1923.

events.label: The Dawes Plan
events.description: American banker Charles Dawes engineered a 1924 plan to reschedule German war reparations and pump U.S. loans into Germany, which paid reparations to Britain and France, which repaid war debts to the U.S. The circular flow held until 1929.

events.label: Immigration Act of 1924 (Johnson–Reed)
events.description: The Johnson–Reed Act set permanent national-origin quotas based on the 1890 census, deliberately cutting southern and eastern European immigration to a trickle and barring almost all Asian immigration entirely. It reshaped American demographics for forty years.

events.label: Indian Citizenship Act
events.description: The Indian Citizenship Act of 1924 granted U.S. citizenship to all Native Americans born in the country, many of whom had been excluded for over a century. Several states still found ways to bar them from voting for decades afterward.

events.label: Coolidge elected in his own right
events.description: Running on 'Keep Cool with Coolidge' and a booming economy, Calvin Coolidge won the 1924 election easily. Robert La Follette's Progressive third-party run drew nearly five million votes but carried only Wisconsin.

events.label: Klan march on Washington
events.description: On August 8, 1925, an estimated 30,000–50,000 hooded Klansmen marched down Pennsylvania Avenue at the organization's peak, when it claimed four to five million members nationwide. It was the high-water mark of the second Klan.

events.label: The Stephenson scandal and Klan collapse
events.description: D.C. Stephenson, the Klan boss who controlled Indiana politics, was convicted of second-degree murder in November 1925 after the death of a woman he had abducted and assaulted. The conviction and his exposure of Klan bribery shattered the movement; membership collapsed within two years.

events.label: The Scopes 'Monkey' Trial
events.description: In July 1925, biology teacher John Scopes was tried in Dayton, Tennessee, for teaching evolution in violation of state law. The clash between Clarence Darrow and William Jennings Bryan, carried live on radio, became the decade's set-piece on fundamentalism versus modernism.

events.label: Death of William Jennings Bryan
events.description: William Jennings Bryan, three-time presidential candidate and the prosecution's star at Scopes, died in his sleep in Dayton five days after the trial ended. His death was widely read as the end of an era of agrarian Protestant populism.

events.label: Aimee Semple McPherson's Angelus Temple
events.description: Evangelist Aimee Semple McPherson opened the 5,300-seat Angelus Temple in Los Angeles in 1923 and built one of the first religious broadcasting empires on her own radio station. Her 1926 disappearance and reappearance became a national tabloid sensation.

events.label: The fundamentalist–modernist controversy
events.description: Conservative Protestants who affirmed 'the fundamentals' — biblical inerrancy, the virgin birth, the literal resurrection — split with modernizers who reconciled faith with science. The fight, named for a 1910s tract series, divided major denominations through the decade.

events.label: The Model T conquers America
events.description: By the mid-1920s Ford's Model T cost under $300, and a moving assembly line built one every 24 seconds. More than 15 million were made by 1927; by then over half the cars on earth were Model Ts, and most American families owned a car.

events.label: The legacy of the five-dollar day
events.description: Ford's 1914 five-dollar day and moving assembly line had shown that paying workers enough to buy what they built was a business strategy. By the twenties, mass production plus mass purchasing power was the model the whole economy ran on.

events.label: Alfred Sloan reorganizes General Motors
events.description: Alfred Sloan took over General Motors and built 'a car for every purse and purpose,' annual model changes, and installment credit through GMAC. By 1927 GM's marketing-driven model overtook Ford's single cheap car.

events.label: Ford's Model A changeover
events.description: Outsold by GM, Ford shut its plants for six months in 1927 to retool from the Model T to the Model A, idling 60,000 workers. The new car drew a million advance orders and showed even Henry Ford had to follow the styling market.

events.label: Route 66 commissioned
events.description: U.S. Route 66, commissioned in November 1926, ran 2,448 miles from Chicago to Los Angeles. The new federal numbered-highway system tied the car culture together and created the roadside economy of motels, diners, and gas stations.

events.label: Federal-Aid Highway Act of 1921
events.description: The 1921 Federal-Aid Highway Act funded a national network of paved interstate roads, matching state spending dollar for dollar. Combined with cheap cars, it launched the suburb, the long-distance truck, and the auto-shaped American landscape.

events.label: The first car suburbs
events.description: Cheap cars and paved roads let the middle class live miles from work for the first time. Developments like Beverly Hills and the Country Club District of Kansas City pioneered the planned, zoned, car-dependent suburb that defined later American growth.

events.label: The electrified home
events.description: By the late twenties nearly two-thirds of American homes had electricity, up from a small minority in 1918, though most farms still had none. Electric light, the refrigerator, the washing machine, and the vacuum cleaner remade domestic life.

events.label: The installment-credit economy
events.description: 'Buy now, pay later' went mainstream in the twenties: by 1927 roughly 15 percent of all retail and the majority of car, radio, and appliance sales were on installment plans. Consumer debt became a structural feature of the American economy for the first time.

events.label: The modern advertising industry
events.description: Madison Avenue agencies industrialized desire, selling not products but status, romance, and fear of inadequacy. Bruce Barton's 1925 bestseller The Man Nobody Knows recast Jesus as the founder of modern business — advertising as the decade's true religion.

events.label: The rise of the chain store
events.description: A&P, Woolworth, and Piggly Wiggly's self-service model spread nationally in the twenties, undercutting local merchants with volume buying. The chain store and the brand replaced the general store and the clerk.

events.label: The radio craze
events.description: Radio set sales jumped from $60 million in 1922 to $850 million in 1929; by decade's end a third of households had a set. For the first time millions of Americans heard the same voice, the same music, and the same ads at the same moment.

events.label: NBC, the first radio network
events.description: RCA launched the National Broadcasting Company in November 1926, the first permanent national radio network, linking dozens of local stations by telephone wire. CBS followed in 1927, creating the broadcast structure that still organizes American media.

events.label: CBS founded
events.description: The Columbia Broadcasting System launched in 1927 and was bought the next year by 26-year-old William S. Paley, who built it into NBC's great rival. Two networks now competed for a national audience.

events.label: Radio Act of 1927
events.description: With the airwaves jammed by interfering stations, the Radio Act of 1927 created the Federal Radio Commission to license broadcasters 'in the public interest.' It established that the spectrum was public property the government would allocate.

events.label: The Hollywood studio system
events.description: By the early twenties the major studios — many founded by Eastern European Jewish immigrants — controlled production, distribution, and theaters from a Los Angeles suburb. Movies became the country's fourth-largest industry and its dominant storyteller.

events.label: The Fatty Arbuckle scandal
events.description: Comedy star Roscoe 'Fatty' Arbuckle was tried three times for the 1921 death of actress Virginia Rappe; the third jury acquitted him and apologized. The scandal ended his career and triggered Hollywood's drive to clean up its image.

events.label: The Hays Office
events.description: To preempt government censorship after a run of scandals, the studios hired Postmaster General Will Hays in 1922 to police movie morality. His office evolved into the Production Code that governed what American films could show until the 1960s.

events.label: Rudolph Valentino, the first heartthrob
events.description: Italian immigrant Rudolph Valentino became the silent screen's great romantic idol with The Sheik (1921). His sudden death in 1926 at 31 set off mass public hysteria and a riot at his New York funeral, the template for modern celebrity mourning.

events.label: Chaplin's The Gold Rush
events.description: Charlie Chaplin's The Gold Rush (1925), with the boot-eating dinner and the dance of the rolls, was the silent era's comic peak and a global success. Chaplin's Tramp was arguably the most recognized human image on earth in the twenties.

events.label: The Jazz Singer and sound film
events.description: Warner Bros.' The Jazz Singer, premiering October 1927 with synchronized song and a few lines of speech, ended the silent era within three years. Its star Al Jolson performed in blackface — the new medium's debut was built on minstrelsy.

events.label: The Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences
events.description: Founded in 1927, the Academy was created partly to head off Hollywood unionization and burnish the industry's prestige. Its awards, first given in 1929, became the movie business's self-coronation.

events.label: The tabloid press
events.description: The New York Daily News (1919) and its imitators built mass circulation on photographs, crime, sex, and sport. The 1928 secret photograph of Ruth Snyder's execution and the Hall–Mills murder trial defined the tabloid's appetite for sensation.

events.label: Babe Ruth and the home-run era
events.description: Babe Ruth's 60 home runs in 1927 capped a career that transformed baseball from a low-scoring contest into a power spectacle and made him the most famous athlete in the world. Yankee Stadium (1923) was 'the House That Ruth Built.'

events.label: The 'Long Count' fight
events.description: Jack Dempsey, the era's great heavyweight, lost to Gene Tunney before 104,000 fans in Chicago in 1927; a disputed long count let Tunney recover from a knockdown. The gate topped $2 million, the first 'million-dollar' era of sport.

events.label: Red Grange turns pro
events.description: Illinois halfback 'Red' Grange, the Galloping Ghost, signed with the Chicago Bears days after his last college game in 1925, drawing 70,000 fans and putting the struggling National Football League on the map.

events.label: Bobby Jones and the golf boom
events.description: Amateur Bobby Jones dominated golf in the twenties, winning thirteen majors and, in 1930, the Grand Slam. Mass-market golf and the country club spread with the suburbs and the new leisure economy.

events.label: Gertrude Ederle swims the Channel
events.description: On August 6, 1926, 20-year-old Gertrude Ederle became the first woman to swim the English Channel, beating the men's record by nearly two hours. Two million people met her ticker-tape parade — proof that a woman could be a national sports hero.

events.label: Lindbergh's solo Atlantic flight
events.description: On May 20–21, 1927, Charles Lindbergh flew the Spirit of St. Louis 3,600 miles from New York to Paris alone in 33.5 hours, the first solo nonstop transatlantic flight. He became the most celebrated person on earth and the symbol of the air age.

events.label: The aviation boom
events.description: Lindbergh's flight set off an 'aviation craze': stock in aircraft companies soared, airmail contracts created the first airlines, and Congress poured money into airports. Aviation became both a real industry and a speculative mania.

events.label: Amelia Earhart crosses the Atlantic
events.description: Amelia Earhart became the first woman to cross the Atlantic by air in 1928, as a passenger, and was instantly made a celebrity by publisher George Putnam. She used the fame to campaign for women in aviation and to plan her own solo flights.

events.label: Louis Armstrong's Hot Five recordings
events.description: Louis Armstrong's 1925–28 Hot Five and Hot Seven records remade jazz around the virtuoso soloist and Armstrong's trumpet and voice. He carried New Orleans jazz to Chicago and then to the world via the phonograph.

events.label: Duke Ellington at the Cotton Club
events.description: Duke Ellington's orchestra began its residency at Harlem's whites-only Cotton Club in December 1927; its nightly radio broadcasts made him a national name. The club epitomized the era's exploitation of Black art for white audiences.

events.label: Bessie Smith, 'Empress of the Blues'
events.description: Bessie Smith's 1923 recordings sold hundreds of thousands of copies into the 'race records' market and made her the highest-paid Black entertainer of the decade. The blues and the phonograph carried Southern Black music to a national audience.

events.label: Jelly Roll Morton and recorded jazz
events.description: Jelly Roll Morton's Red Hot Peppers recordings (1926–27) were among the first to fully arrange jazz, fixing the New Orleans style on record. The phonograph and radio were spreading a regional Black music nationwide within a few years.

events.label: Gershwin's Rhapsody in Blue
events.description: George Gershwin premiered Rhapsody in Blue in New York in February 1924, an attempt to fuse jazz with the concert hall. It signaled jazz crossing into mainstream and 'respectable' American music.

events.label: Locke's The New Negro
events.description: Alain Locke's 1925 anthology The New Negro became the manifesto of the Harlem Renaissance, gathering Hughes, Hurston, McKay, and others to argue that Black art was remaking American culture from Harlem outward.

events.label: Langston Hughes, The Weary Blues
events.description: Langston Hughes published The Weary Blues in 1926 and his manifesto 'The Negro Artist and the Racial Mountain,' insisting Black writers draw on jazz, the blues, and Black vernacular without apology. He became the Harlem Renaissance's defining poet.

events.label: Zora Neale Hurston in Harlem
events.description: Zora Neale Hurston arrived in Harlem in 1925, trained as an anthropologist under Franz Boas, and began collecting Southern Black folklore. Her work fused fieldwork and fiction and became central to the renaissance and to later American letters.

events.label: Claude McKay's protest verse
events.description: Jamaican-born Claude McKay's militant 1919 sonnet 'If We Must Die,' written after the Red Summer, and his 1922 collection Harlem Shadows gave the renaissance its angriest early voice. His 1928 novel Home to Harlem was a bestseller.

events.label: The Cotton Club
events.description: The Cotton Club, opened in Harlem in 1923 by a bootlegger, presented Black performers to whites-only audiences in 'jungle'-themed shows. It was the most famous nightclub of the age and a symbol of how the Jazz Age commodified Black culture.

events.label: The NAACP anti-lynching campaign
events.description: The NAACP pushed the Dyer Anti-Lynching Bill through the House in 1922; a Senate filibuster by Southern Democrats killed it, as it would kill every such bill for decades. The campaign nonetheless made lynching a national political issue.

events.label: A. Philip Randolph and the Pullman porters
events.description: A. Philip Randolph founded the Brotherhood of Sleeping Car Porters in 1925, organizing the Black men who staffed Pullman railroad cars. It became the first Black-led union to win a major contract and a training ground for the later civil-rights movement.

events.label: Garvey convicted and deported
events.description: Marcus Garvey was convicted of mail fraud connected to his Black Star steamship line in 1923, imprisoned, and deported to Jamaica in 1927. The collapse of the largest Black mass movement of the era was a lasting blow and a long controversy.

events.label: Fitzgerald's This Side of Paradise
events.description: F. Scott Fitzgerald's debut novel This Side of Paradise (1920) made him famous at 23 and defined the 'flapper' and the disillusioned postwar youth. He coined the phrase 'the Jazz Age' for the decade he became its chronicler of.

events.label: The Great Gatsby published
events.description: F. Scott Fitzgerald's The Great Gatsby (1925) turned the decade's bootleggers, new money, and hollow glamour into the era's defining novel. It sold modestly in the twenties and was rediscovered as an American masterpiece after Fitzgerald's death.

events.label: Hemingway's The Sun Also Rises
events.description: Ernest Hemingway's The Sun Also Rises (1926) gave the 'Lost Generation' of postwar expatriates its portrait and a stripped-down prose style that reshaped American writing. Its epigraph from Gertrude Stein named the generation.

events.label: The Lost Generation in Paris
events.description: Hemingway, Fitzgerald, Stein, Pound, and others gathered in 1920s Paris, alienated by the war and Prohibition-era America. The expatriate scene around Gertrude Stein's salon and Sylvia Beach's bookshop produced much of modern American literature.

events.label: H. L. Mencken and The American Mercury
events.description: H. L. Mencken, the decade's most quoted critic, co-founded The American Mercury in 1924 and skewered the 'booboisie,' Prohibition, fundamentalism, and the Klan. He coined 'the Bible Belt' and covered Scopes as cultural warfare.

events.label: The Algonquin Round Table
events.description: Dorothy Parker, Robert Benchley, and a circle of New York wits lunched daily at the Algonquin Hotel through the early twenties, their quips amplified by newspaper columns. They embodied the decade's urbane, sardonic literary celebrity.

events.label: The flapper and the 'New Woman'
events.description: Bobbed hair, raised hemlines, cigarettes, cosmetics, and the Charleston marked the 'flapper,' the decade's image of liberated young womanhood. The reality was more limited than the image, but the public revolt against Victorian constraint was real.

events.label: The Charleston
events.description: Popularized by the 1923 Black musical Runnin' Wild, the Charleston became the decade's defining dance craze, scandalizing elders and filling speakeasies. Like the music it moved to, it traveled from Black culture into the white mainstream.

events.label: Speakeasies and bootlegging
events.description: Prohibition created tens of thousands of illegal bars — 'speakeasies' — and a vast smuggling and home-distilling economy. By the mid-twenties New York alone had an estimated 30,000–100,000 speakeasies, more drinking spots than before the ban.

events.label: Rum Row and the bootleg supply chain
events.description: Ships anchored on 'Rum Row' just outside the three-mile limit, fast boats ran liquor ashore, and Detroit's Purple Gang ferried Canadian whiskey across the river. Bootlegging built fortunes, fast cars, and the organized smuggling networks of the modern Mafia.

events.label: Prohibition and organized crime
events.description: The illegal liquor trade handed criminal gangs a multibillion-dollar industry and the money to corrupt police, courts, and city hall. The national crime syndicate of the 1930s was built on twenties bootleg profits.

events.label: Al Capone runs Chicago
events.description: Al Capone took over the Chicago Outfit in 1925 and built a bootlegging, gambling, and vice empire grossing an estimated $60 million a year. He effectively controlled the city's police and politicians and became the era's celebrity gangster.

events.label: St. Valentine's Day Massacre
events.description: On February 14, 1929, Capone gunmen disguised as police machine-gunned seven members of Bugs Moran's North Side gang against a Chicago garage wall. The shock of the massacre turned public opinion decisively against Capone and Prohibition.

events.label: Prohibition's failure becomes undeniable
events.description: By the mid-twenties poisoned industrial alcohol was killing thousands, the government was deliberately denaturing it, juries refused to convict, and 'wet' politics was rising. Prohibition's collapse was visible years before its 1933 repeal.

events.label: 'The business of America is business'
events.description: In a January 1925 speech Coolidge said 'the chief business of the American people is business,' summing up an administration that cut taxes, slashed regulation, and trusted prosperity to private enterprise. The line became the decade's motto.

events.label: The 1926 Revenue Act
events.description: The Revenue Act of 1926 cut the top income-tax rate to 25 percent and gutted the estate tax, the centerpiece of Mellon's program. Revenues stayed high through the boom, seeming to vindicate the strategy until 1929.

events.label: The farm depression of the twenties
events.description: Farm prices collapsed after wartime demand vanished and never recovered; a quarter of farmers lost their land in the twenties. Congress twice passed the McNary–Haugen farm-relief bill; Coolidge vetoed it both times. The countryside never got the boom.

events.label: The 'sick industries'
events.description: Coal, textiles, and New England manufacturing stagnated or shrank through the boom as oil, the South, and synthetics undercut them. The 1929 Loray Mill strike in Gastonia, North Carolina, exposed how little prosperity reached the textile South.

events.label: Taylorism and the efficiency cult
events.description: Frederick Taylor's 'scientific management' — time-and-motion study, the stopwatch, the standardized task — spread from the factory floor into offices, schools, and homes in the twenties. Efficiency became a national value and a household ideology.

events.label: Freud's American vogue
events.description: Popularized (and simplified) psychoanalysis swept twenties America: the 'inferiority complex,' the unconscious, and 'repression' entered everyday speech and advertising. Edward Bernays applied his uncle Freud's ideas to selling and called it public relations.

events.label: Eugenics and Buck v. Bell
events.description: Eugenics — the pseudoscience of breeding 'better' humans — was mainstream in the twenties, taught in schools and written into the 1924 immigration law. In Buck v. Bell (1927) the Supreme Court upheld forced sterilization: 'Three generations of imbeciles are enough.'

events.label: The Butler Act
events.description: Tennessee's Butler Act, signed in March 1925, made it illegal to teach in public schools 'any theory that denies the story of the divine creation of man.' The ACLU's search for a teacher to challenge it set up the Scopes trial.

events.label: Darrow cross-examines Bryan
events.description: On the trial's seventh day, defense lawyer Clarence Darrow put prosecutor William Jennings Bryan on the stand as an expert on the Bible and exposed his literalism to ridicule before a national radio audience. It was the trial's decisive scene.

events.label: Billy Sunday's revival circuit
events.description: Former baseball player Billy Sunday was the most famous revivalist of the era, preaching a muscular, anti-modern, pro-Prohibition Christianity to millions in purpose-built wooden tabernacles. He embodied the populist Protestant resistance to the Jazz Age.

events.label: The Florida land boom
events.description: From 1923 to 1925 a speculative frenzy in Florida real estate drove lots in Miami and Coral Gables to absurd prices, often bought sight-unseen with 'binder' down payments and flipped within weeks. It was the dress rehearsal for the stock bubble.

events.label: The 1926 Miami hurricane
events.description: The Great Miami Hurricane of September 1926 killed hundreds and devastated the city, finishing off the already-deflating Florida land boom. Speculators were wiped out; the speculative money moved north to Wall Street.

events.label: Great Mississippi Flood of 1927
events.description: The 1927 Mississippi flood inundated 27,000 square miles, displaced over 600,000 people, and fell hardest on Black sharecroppers conscripted into the levee work. Commerce Secretary Herbert Hoover's relief role helped make him president.

events.label: The skyscraper race
events.description: New developers competed to build the world's tallest building as a corporate billboard. The contest between the Bank of Manhattan at 40 Wall Street, the Chrysler Building, and the planned Empire State Building defined the late-twenties skyline.

events.label: The Chrysler Building rises
events.description: Begun in 1928, the Art Deco Chrysler Building briefly became the world's tallest in 1930 by secretly assembling a 185-foot spire inside the dome and raising it in 90 minutes to beat 40 Wall Street. It is the decade's signature building.

events.label: Art Deco in America
events.description: The streamlined, geometric Art Deco style, launched at a 1925 Paris exposition, swept American skyscrapers, theaters, radios, and cars. It was the visual signature of the machine-age, consumer-age decade.

events.label: The movie palaces
events.description: Lavish 'movie palaces' seating thousands — Roxy's 1927 New York theater held 5,920 — turned moviegoing into an opulent mass ritual. By 1929 some 80 million Americans, two-thirds of the country, went to the movies every week.

events.label: The stadium-building boom
events.description: Yankee Stadium (1923), Soldier Field (1924), the Rose Bowl (1922), and dozens of college stadiums were built as concrete monuments to the new mass spectator sport. Sport became architecture and big business in the same decade.

events.label: Al Smith and anti-Catholicism
events.description: New York Governor Al Smith, the first Catholic nominated for president by a major party, was the Democrats' 1928 candidate. The campaign unleashed open anti-Catholic bigotry, whispering that the Pope would run the country from Rome.

events.label: Herbert Hoover elected
events.description: Herbert Hoover, the celebrated engineer and Commerce secretary, beat Al Smith in a 1928 landslide, promising 'a final triumph over poverty' and 'a chicken in every pot.' He took office in March 1929, seven months before the Crash.

events.label: The great bull market
events.description: Stock prices roughly doubled between early 1928 and September 1929 on a flood of optimism and borrowed money. 'Everybody ought to be rich,' a Democratic financier wrote in 1929; shoeshine boys gave stock tips.

events.label: Buying on margin
events.description: Investors bought stock with as little as 10 percent down, borrowing the rest from brokers ('on margin'). It multiplied gains on the way up and guaranteed forced selling on the way down; margin debt hit $8.5 billion by 1929.

events.label: Investment trusts and leverage
events.description: Investment trusts — companies that owned shares in other companies, often layered on borrowed money — proliferated in 1928–29. The pyramided leverage that magnified gains made the eventual collapse far worse.

events.label: The Federal Reserve and the bubble
events.description: The Federal Reserve, the central bank created in 1913, debated through 1928–29 whether to raise interest rates to choke speculation without crushing the economy. It moved late and indecisively, and the debate over its role never ended.

events.label: The market peaks
events.description: The Dow Jones Industrial Average hit its 1920s high of 381 on September 3, 1929, then drifted down through September and early October as nervousness grew. Most observers still expected only a 'healthy correction.'

events.label: Black Thursday
events.description: On Thursday, October 24, 1929, a wave of panic selling hit the New York Stock Exchange; a banker pool's show of support steadied prices that afternoon. The reprieve lasted one weekend.

events.label: Black Tuesday and the Crash
events.description: On Tuesday, October 29, 1929, 16 million shares were dumped in the worst day in the exchange's history; the ticker ran hours behind. Roughly $30 billion in paper wealth vanished within two weeks, ending the decade and the illusion.

events.label: The morning after the Crash
events.description: Hoover and the bankers insisted prosperity was 'just around the corner'; the market briefly rallied into 1930 before collapsing for real. The Crash did not cause the Great Depression alone, but it ended the Roaring Twenties — and the next era begins here.

events.label: Jim Crow at its height
events.description: By 1918 the Southern system of legal segregation, Black disfranchisement, and lynching built after Reconstruction was fully entrenched. It was the engine of the Great Migration and the backdrop to every Black story of the decade.

events.label: The Progressive Era's legacy
events.description: The 1900s–1910s reform wave gave the twenties the income tax, the Federal Reserve, direct election of senators, women's suffrage, and Prohibition. The decade lived off Progressive machinery while repudiating Progressive politics.

events.label: The Gilded Age inheritance
events.description: The post-1877 decades of railroads, steel, oil, mass immigration, and the great trusts built the industrial machine the twenties consumer economy ran on. The Gilded Age made the factories; the twenties learned how to sell their output to everyone.

events.label: America's World War
events.description: The United States entered the World War in April 1917, sent two million men to France, and emerged in 1918 as the world's leading creditor and industrial power, owed billions by its allies. The war made America rich and turned it inward.

events.label: Wartime repression and its hangover
events.description: The 1917 Espionage and 1918 Sedition Acts jailed antiwar socialists like Eugene Debs and built the surveillance apparatus turned on radicals after the war. The Red Scare ran on machinery the war had already installed.

events.label: Harding pardons Eugene Debs
events.description: Socialist leader Eugene Debs, jailed under the Sedition Act for an antiwar speech and a 1920 presidential candidate from his prison cell, was released by Harding's commutation at Christmas 1921. It signaled the Red Scare's exhaustion.

events.label: The Teapot Dome trials
events.description: Albert Fall was convicted of bribery in 1929, the first U.S. cabinet officer imprisoned for crimes in office. The oilman who bribed him was acquitted of bribing him — a verdict that became a national joke about justice for the rich.

events.label: Sacco and Vanzetti executed
events.description: After seven years of appeals and worldwide protest, Nicola Sacco and Bartolomeo Vanzetti were electrocuted in Massachusetts on August 23, 1927. Whether they were guilty is still argued; that the trial was unfair is the scholarly consensus.

events.label: The Kellogg–Briand Pact
events.description: In August 1928 the U.S. and France led 15 nations in signing a pact 'renouncing war as an instrument of national policy.' Toothless but earnest, it captured the decade's war-weary idealism and its retreat from world affairs.

events.label: Asian exclusion completed
events.description: The 1924 act's 'aliens ineligible to citizenship' clause barred essentially all immigration from Japan and the rest of Asia, extending the 1882 Chinese Exclusion Act to the whole continent. It poisoned U.S.–Japan relations for a generation.

events.label: Nativism and '100% Americanism'
events.description: Postwar nativism fused anti-immigrant, anti-Catholic, anti-radical, and anti-Black feeling into a '100% American' movement that powered the Klan, immigration restriction, and the Red Scare. It was the decade's organizing political emotion outside the cities.

events.label: The Muscle Shoals fight
events.description: Senator George Norris fought through the twenties to keep the government's wartime Muscle Shoals dam and nitrate plant public; Coolidge and Hoover wanted it sold to private interests like Henry Ford. The deadlocked fight set up the later public-power debate.

events.label: Anti-evolution laws spread
events.description: Despite the ridicule of Scopes, Mississippi and Arkansas passed their own anti-evolution laws (1926, 1928), and textbook publishers quietly dropped Darwin for decades. The 'modernists' won the press; the fundamentalists won the curriculum.

events.label: 'I do not choose to run'
events.description: On August 2, 1927, the fourth anniversary of his accession, Coolidge handed reporters a single sentence: 'I do not choose to run for President in nineteen twenty eight.' He left office before the boom he presided over collapsed.

events.label: Politics on the air
events.description: The 1924 conventions and campaign were the first broadcast nationally; Coolidge's flat radio voice suited the medium better than the old stump orators'. Radio began reshaping how Americans chose presidents.

events.label: The 103-ballot Democratic convention
events.description: The 1924 Democratic convention took a record 103 ballots over 16 days, deadlocked between the Klan-backed William McAdoo and the Catholic Al Smith, with a motion to condemn the Klan by name failing by one vote. It showed the Klan's grip on national politics.

events.label: Religious broadcasting begins
events.description: Aimee Semple McPherson, Father Charles Coughlin, and others pioneered religious radio in the twenties, reaching audiences no church building could. The marriage of evangelism and broadcasting started here and never ended.

events.label: The Air Commerce Act
events.description: The Air Commerce Act of 1926 gave the federal government authority to license pilots and aircraft and build airways, creating the regulatory base of commercial aviation just as Lindbergh's flight set off the boom.

events.label: Television's first demonstrations
events.description: Philo Farnsworth transmitted the first electronic television image in 1927 and AT&T demonstrated TV in 1927; the technology that would dominate the next era was already being born inside the radio decade.

events.label: Goddard's liquid-fuel rocket
events.description: On March 16, 1926, Robert Goddard launched the world's first liquid-fueled rocket from a Massachusetts farm; it flew 41 feet. Ridiculed at the time, it was the seed of twentieth-century rocketry.

events.label: Insulin and the medical twenties
events.description: Insulin, first used on a patient in 1922, turned diabetes from a death sentence into a managed disease and signaled the decade's real scientific advance amid the boom. Medicine, like industry, was being transformed by the laboratory.

events.label: The first nationally broadcast trial
events.description: Chicago's WGN ran telephone lines to Dayton and broadcast the Scopes trial live, the first U.S. trial carried on radio. The culture war was now a national broadcast event, watched in real time across the country.

events.label: The Osage murders
events.description: Wealthy from oil under their Oklahoma land, dozens of Osage were murdered in the early twenties by whites scheming to inherit their headrights. The new Bureau of Investigation's case helped build J. Edgar Hoover's FBI.

events.label: The Snyder–Gray murder trial
events.description: The 1927 trial of Ruth Snyder and Judd Gray for murdering her husband was a tabloid sensation; a photographer's hidden camera caught her execution for the Daily News front page. It captured the decade's appetite for crime as mass entertainment.

events.label: The machinery of celebrity
events.description: Lindbergh's flight showed how radio, tabloids, newsreels, and ghostwritten books could manufacture a global celebrity overnight. Fame itself became an industry in the twenties, with Babe Ruth, Valentino, and Lindbergh as its prototypes.

events.label: Women in the modern workforce
events.description: More women worked for wages in the twenties — as typists, telephone operators, clerks, and salesgirls — but mostly in low-paid 'pink-collar' jobs, and most quit at marriage. The vote had not delivered economic equality.

events.label: Margaret Sanger's birth-control movement
events.description: Margaret Sanger opened the first legal U.S. birth-control clinic in 1923 and built a movement that was genuinely liberatory for women and, like much of the era's reform, entangled with eugenics. Family size fell sharply in the urban twenties.

events.label: How the car remade society
events.description: By the late twenties the car had reshaped dating, churchgoing, vacations, crime, and city form; sociologists studying 'Middletown' in 1929 found families would 'go without clothes' before giving up the automobile.

events.label: The Middletown study
events.description: Robert and Helen Lynd's 1929 sociological study of Muncie, Indiana, documented how the car, the radio, advertising, and the factory had transformed an ordinary American town in a single generation. It is the decade's self-portrait.

events.label: Fads and crazes
events.description: Mah-jongg, crossword puzzles, flagpole sitting, dance marathons, and Coué's 'every day in every way' auto-suggestion swept the decade. Mass media could now make a national craze out of almost anything within weeks.

events.label: Jazz goes national
events.description: Radio, phonograph records, and the Great Migration carried New Orleans and Chicago jazz to white dance halls, college campuses, and Europe within a decade. Older critics called it degenerate; the young made it the sound of modern life.

events.label: Paul Whiteman, the 'King of Jazz'
events.description: Bandleader Paul Whiteman sold a polished, orchestrated 'symphonic jazz' to mass white audiences and was crowned the 'King of Jazz' — a marketing of Black music that made him rich while its originators went largely unpaid.

events.label: The rise of college culture
events.description: College enrollment doubled in the twenties, and the campus — football, fraternities, raccoon coats, the 'collegiate' style — became a mass cultural model. Higher education was becoming a middle-class expectation, not an elite preserve.

events.label: The Anti-Saloon League's power
events.description: The Anti-Saloon League, the most effective single-issue lobby in American history to that point, had pushed Prohibition through and policed dry politicians for a decade. Its grip showed how an organized minority could write the Constitution.

events.label: The Wickersham Commission begins
events.description: Hoover appointed the Wickersham Commission in 1929 to study Prohibition enforcement; its 1931 report documented its failure while bizarrely recommending it continue. By decade's end even the government could not defend the law.

events.label: Hoover's 'associative state'
events.description: As Commerce secretary from 1921 to 1928, Herbert Hoover built an activist department that promoted standardization, trade associations, radio regulation, and aviation. The decade's most dynamic administrator believed government should organize, not run, the economy.

events.label: The National Origins quota takes effect
events.description: The full National Origins Formula took effect in July 1929, fixing immigration at a total of about 150,000 a year apportioned to favor Britain and Germany. It would shape American immigration until 1965.

events.label: Amos 'n' Andy and network programming
events.description: Amos 'n' Andy, two white performers in dialect, debuted in 1928 and became radio's first national hit, drawing audiences that halted other activity. Network radio's first blockbuster was built, like The Jazz Singer, on minstrel caricature.

events.label: Mickey Mouse debuts
events.description: Walt Disney's Steamboat Willie, premiering November 1928, introduced Mickey Mouse and synchronized cartoon sound. A new American mass-entertainment empire began in the same months as the bull market's final surge.

events.label: Show Boat changes the musical
events.description: Kern and Hammerstein's Show Boat (December 1927) wove a serious story of race and miscegenation into a Broadway musical, with 'Ol' Man River' sung by a Black performer. It pointed the American musical toward drama, not just revue.

events.label: An American modernism
events.description: Georgia O'Keeffe, Edward Hopper, and the Stieglitz circle built a distinctly American modern art in the twenties, while Harlem's Aaron Douglas gave the renaissance its visual style. The decade asked what American culture, freed from Europe, looked like.

events.label: Women, drink, and Prohibition's reversal
events.description: Pauline Sabin's Women's Organization for National Prohibition Reform, founded 1929, turned the argument that women wanted Prohibition on its head and gave repeal a respectable face. The dry coalition was cracking from inside.

events.label: The ignored warnings
events.description: Economist Roger Babson predicted a crash in September 1929 and was mocked; Yale's Irving Fisher countered that stocks had reached 'a permanently high plateau' days before the Crash. The experts who reassured the public were the ones believed.

events.label: The banking system's hidden fragility
events.description: Beneath the boom, 25,000 mostly small, undiversified, unit banks operated with no deposit insurance. The structure that made the Crash become a depression was built into the twenties financial system long before October 1929.

events.label: The Young Plan
events.description: The 1929 Young Plan again rescheduled German reparations and depended on continued American lending. When the Crash cut off U.S. capital, the circular debt structure of the twenties collapsed and pulled down the world economy.

events.label: The American Legion founded
events.description: Veterans of the World War founded the American Legion in 1919; it became a powerful, conservative, anti-radical lobby and a fixture of the era's '100% American' politics, while also pressing for the soldiers' bonus that would explode in 1932.

events.label: The Centralia massacre
events.description: On Armistice Day 1919, an American Legion parade and the radical Industrial Workers of the World clashed in Centralia, Washington; the gunfight and the lynching of the IWW member Wesley Everest fused the Red Scare with veteran-led violence.

events.label: The West Virginia mine wars
events.description: The 1921 Battle of Blair Mountain pitted some 10,000 striking armed coal miners against company gunmen and federal troops in the largest labor uprising in U.S. history, ending only when the Army intervened. The 'sick' coal regions never saw the boom.

events.label: The 1922 railway shopmen's strike
events.description: Some 400,000 railway shop workers struck in 1922 against wage cuts; a sweeping federal injunction broke it and signaled the decade's hostile turn against organized labor. Union membership fell through the prosperous twenties.

events.label: The Taft Court and business
events.description: Chief Justice William Howard Taft's Supreme Court (1921–30) struck down child-labor laws and minimum-wage laws and limited union power, writing the decade's pro-business ideology into constitutional law. It was the legal arm of 'the business of America.'

events.label: The immigrant city before the door closed
events.description: The 1920 census was the first to find most Americans living in cities, many of them densely packed immigrant neighborhoods built by the pre-1924 waves. The twenties closed the door on the system that had made those cities.

events.label: The Rosewood massacre
events.description: In January 1923 a white mob destroyed the Black town of Rosewood, Florida, after a false assault accusation, killing an uncertain number and driving out the survivors for good. It was one of several twenties race massacres long erased from public memory.

events.label: Black institutions in the Jim Crow twenties
events.description: Black colleges, the Black press, the church, and Black-owned businesses expanded in the twenties, North and South, building the institutional base that the civil-rights movement would later mobilize. Survival under Jim Crow was itself organized.

events.label: Transcontinental air mail
events.description: Regular coast-to-coast air mail began in 1924, cutting cross-country delivery to about 30 hours. The 1925 Kelly Act handed the routes to private carriers, founding the companies that became United, American, and TWA.

events.label: A law for the poor
events.description: The rich kept stocked cellars and drank at private clubs; Prohibition fell hardest on working people and immigrants whose saloons were raided and whose bootleg liquor was deadliest. The class double standard fueled the case for repeal.

events.label: The Jones Law stiffens Prohibition
events.description: The 1929 Jones Law made first-offense bootlegging a felony with up to five years and $10,000 in penalties, the dry movement's last hardening of the law just as public support collapsed. Within four years Prohibition was repealed.

events.label: Stock pools and insider rigging
events.description: Operators secretly pooled money to pump a stock with rumor and wash trades, then dumped it on the public — perfectly legal in the twenties. The unregulated, manipulable market of the decade is why the New Deal created the SEC afterward.

events.label: RCA, the bull market's glamour stock
events.description: Radio Corporation of America rose from about $85 to $549 in 1928 alone without ever paying a dividend, the decade's emblem of a price driven by the future people imagined rather than earnings. It cratered in the Crash.

events.label: The Empire State Building announced
events.description: Plans for the 102-story Empire State Building were unveiled in 1929 to top the Chrysler Building; ground broke in 1930. Conceived at the peak of the boom, it opened into the Depression two-thirds empty, nicknamed the 'Empty State Building.'

events.label: The car and the national parks
events.description: Auto tourism transformed the national parks in the twenties: visitor numbers multiplied, the Park Service built roads and lodges, and the cross-country motor trip became a middle-class ritual. Leisure itself was becoming an industry.

events.label: The Mer Rouge Klan murders
events.description: In August 1921 hooded men in Mer Rouge, Louisiana, abducted, tortured, and murdered two local men who had spoken against the Ku Klux Klan; their crushed bodies surfaced in a lake months later. No one was convicted because the Klan controlled the local courts, a stark illustration that the 'respectable' second Klan was also a violent terror organization.

events.label: Sylvia Beach publishes Ulysses
events.description: In 1922 the American expatriate Sylvia Beach published James Joyce's Ulysses from her Paris bookshop, Shakespeare and Company, after every commercial house refused it and U.S. obscenity law banned it. Her shop was the clubhouse of the Lost Generation expatriates and her gamble became a landmark of literary modernism.

events.label: Frankfurter and the report on Justice Department lawlessness
events.description: In 1920 a group of prominent lawyers including the Harvard professor and future Supreme Court justice Felix Frankfurter published a report documenting the illegal practices of the Justice Department in the Palmer Raids. It helped turn elite opinion against the Red Scare and stands as an early defense of civil liberties.

events.label: The end of the Roaring Twenties
events.description: The decade that had named itself for its own exuberance ended in eleven days of October 1929. The Depression and the next world war would reshape American society, and returning Black veterans would help spark the civil-rights movement that transformed the country again.
